digest:
## 🔬 Major Technical Developments

### C++26 is done: ISO C++ standards meeting Trip Report
URL: https://herbsutter.com/2026/03/29/c26-is-done-trip-report-march-2026-iso-c-standards-meeting-london-croydon-uk/
C++26 formally ratified — the language modernization cycle continues on schedule
The latest standard lands reflection, contracts, and improved concurrency primitives. For infrastructure-heavy shops still running C++ at scale (think HFT, game engines, embedded systems), this signals continued viability of the language against Rust encroachment. Watch for toolchain vendor adoption timelines as the real implementation bottleneck.

### ChatGPT won't let you type until Cloudflare reads your React state
URL: https://www.buchodi.com/chatgpt-wont-let-you-type-until-cloudflare-reads-your-react-state-i-decrypted-the-program-that-does-it/
OpenAI's anti-bot defenses now inspect client-side application state before allowing interaction
This is aggressive browser fingerprinting at the application layer, not just network layer. Indicates OpenAI is fighting significant API abuse and scraping — useful signal on the gray market for model access. Also raises questions about what telemetry major AI providers are collecting from legitimate users.

### Making HNSW actually work with WHERE clauses
URL: https://cigrainger.com/blog/duckdb-hnsw-acorn/
DuckDB vector search gets practical filtered retrieval via ACORN algorithm
The gap between vector database benchmarks and production RAG systems has always been filtered queries. This DuckDB extension addresses the core problem of "find similar vectors WHERE metadata = X" without brute-force fallback. Relevant for any AI infrastructure play — vector DB differentiation is increasingly about hybrid query performance.

## 💼 Business & Market Signals

### Sora's shutdown could be a reality check moment for AI video
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/29/soras-shutdown-could-be-a-reality-check-moment-for-ai-video/
OpenAI pulls back on video generation product — compute economics may not pencil
After the Sora hype cycle, this shutdown suggests either unit economics issues or strategic reallocation toward more defensible products. The broader AI video space (Runway, Pika, et al.) should be watched for similar contractions. Generative video may be entering its "AI winter" phase while text and code agents dominate investment.

### 'Project Hail Mary' becomes Amazon MGM's biggest box office hit
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/29/project-hail-mary-becomes-amazon-mgms-biggest-box-office-hit/
Amazon's theatrical strategy validated with sci-fi blockbuster performance
The MGM acquisition thesis included theatrical optionality — this is the first major proof point. Relevant for media/streaming competitive dynamics: Amazon proving it can compete in theatrical windows changes negotiating leverage with talent and distributors. Watch for increased theatrical-first commitments from Prime Video.

### YouTube CEO says the best YouTubers will 'never leave their home'
URL: https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/29/youtube-ceo-says-the-best-youtubers-will-never-leave-their-home/
Mohan dismisses Netflix creator poaching threat — doubling down on native-format content
This is positioning against Netflix's creator deals and signals YouTube believes its algorithmic distribution moat is wider than production budgets. The "never leave their home" framing suggests YouTube sees direct-to-camera, high-frequency content as its defensible niche versus scripted competition.

## 📋 Case Studies & Deep Dives

### Voyager 1 runs on 69 KB of memory and an 8-track tape recorder
URL: https://techfixated.com/a-1977-time-capsule-voyager-1-runs-on-69-kb-of-memory-and-an-8-track-tape-recorder-4/
47-year-old spacecraft still operating on hardware that predates the personal computer
Useful case study in extreme constraint engineering and graceful degradation. The mission's continued operation despite failed components offers lessons for long-duration autonomous systems — relevant framing for space infrastructure investments and edge computing reliability discussions.

### Coding agents could make free software matter again
URL: https://www.gjlondon.com/blog/ai-agents-could-make-free-software-matter-again/
AI coding assistants may shift OSS economics by collapsing maintenance costs
The argument: if agents can handle the grunt work of dependency updates, security patches, and documentation, volunteer-maintained projects become viable again. This has implications for enterprise software procurement — the buy-vs-build calculus changes if "build" includes an AI maintenance layer. Worth tracking which OSS projects get agent-augmented first.

### My MacBook keyboard is broken and it's insanely expensive to fix
URL: https://tobiasberg.net/posts/my-macbook-keyboard-is-broken-and-its-insanely-expensive-to-fix/
Apple repair economics remain punitive — $500+ keyboard replacements on premium hardware
Right-to-repair tailwind. Apple's integrated design philosophy creates replacement cost structures that push users toward new device purchases rather than repairs. Regulatory pressure in EU and state-level US legislation may force design changes, creating potential margin compression in Apple's services/hardware flywheel.

## ⚡ Signal vs. Noise

The Sora shutdown alongside the AI coding agents piece reveals the current AI market structure: video generation is hitting compute-to-value ceilings while developer tooling remains the most defensible category. Meanwhile, legacy infrastructure stories (Voyager, C++26, Apple repair costs) remind us that the installed base moves slowly — opportunities exist in both greenfield AI plays and brownfield optimization. The real alpha is in hybrid systems that bridge the gap.
